Lightly touching the string with a fingertip at a harmonic [node](/wiki/Node_(physics) "Node (physics)"), but without fully pressing the string, and then plucking or bowing the string, creates [harmonics](/wiki/Harmonic "Harmonic"). Instead of the normal tone, a higher pitched note sounds. Each node is at an integer division of the string, for example half-way or one-third along the length of the string. A responsive instrument will sound numerous possible harmonic nodes along the length of the string. Harmonics are marked in music either with a little circle above the note that determines the pitch of the harmonic, or by diamond-shaped note heads. There are two types of harmonics: *natural harmonics* and *[artificial harmonics](/wiki/Artificial_harmonic "Artificial harmonic")* (also known as *false harmonics*).

Natural harmonics are played on an open string. The pitch of the open string when it is plucked or bowed is called the fundamental frequency. Harmonics are also called *overtones* or *partials*. They occur at whole-number multiples of the fundamental, which is called the first harmonic. The second harmonic is the first [overtone](/wiki/Overtone "Overtone") (the octave above the open string), the third harmonic is the second overtone, and so on. The second harmonic is in the middle of the string and sounds an octave higher than the string's pitch. The third harmonic breaks the string into thirds and sounds an octave and a fifth above the fundamental, and the fourth harmonic breaks the string into quarters sounding two octaves above the first. The sound of the second harmonic is the clearest of them all, because it is a common [node](/wiki/Node_(physics) "Node (physics)") with all the succeeding even-numbered harmonics (4th, 6th, etc.). The third and succeeding odd-numbered harmonics are harder to play because they break the string into an odd number of vibrating parts and do not share as many nodes with other harmonics.

Artificial harmonics are more difficult to produce than natural harmonics, as they involve both stopping the string and playing a harmonic on the stopped note. Using the *octave frame* (the normal distance between the first and fourth fingers in any given position) with the fourth finger just touching the string a [fourth](/wiki/Interval_(music) "Interval (music)") higher than the stopped note produces the fourth harmonic, two octaves above the stopped note. Finger placement and pressure, as well as bow speed, pressure, and sounding point are all essential in getting the desired harmonic to sound. And to add to the challenge, in passages with different notes played as false harmonics, the distance between stopping finger and harmonic finger must constantly change, since the spacing between notes changes along the length of the string.

The *harmonic finger* can also touch at a [major third](/wiki/Interval_(music) "Interval (music)") above the pressed note (the fifth harmonic), or a [fifth](/wiki/Interval_(music) "Interval (music)") higher (a third harmonic). These harmonics are less commonly used; in the case of the major third, both the stopped note and touched note must be played slightly sharp otherwise the harmonic does not speak as readily. In the case of the fifth, the stretch is greater than is comfortable for many violinists. In the general repertoire fractions smaller than a sixth are not used. However, divisions up to an eighth are sometimes used and, given a good instrument and a skilled player, divisions as small as a twelfth are possible. There are a few books dedicated solely to the study of violin harmonics. Two comprehensive works are Henryk Heller's seven-volume *Theory of Harmonics*, published by [Simrock](/wiki/N._Simrock "N. Simrock") in 1928, and Michelangelo Abbado's five-volume *Tecnica dei suoni armonici* published by Ricordi in 1934.

Elaborate passages in artificial harmonics can be found in virtuoso violin literature, especially of the 19th and early 20th centuries. Two notable examples of this are an entire section of [Vittorio Monti](/wiki/Vittorio_Monti "Vittorio Monti")'s *[Csárdás](/wiki/Cs%C3%A1rd%C3%A1s_(Monti) "Csárdás (Monti)")* and a passage towards the middle of the third movement of [Pyotr Ilyich Tchaikovsky's Violin Concerto](/wiki/Violin_Concerto_(Tchaikovsky) "Violin Concerto (Tchaikovsky)"). A section of the third movement of [Paganini's Violin Concerto No. 1](/wiki/Violin_Concerto_No._1_(Paganini) "Violin Concerto No. 1 (Paganini)") consists of [double-stopped](/wiki/Double-stop "Double-stop") thirds in harmonics.

When strings are worn, dirty and old, the harmonics may no longer be accurate in pitch. For this reason, violinists change their strings regularly.

### Right hand and tone color

The strings may be sounded by drawing the hair of the bow held by the right hand across them (*arco*) or by plucking them (*[pizzicato](/wiki/Pizzicato "Pizzicato")*) most often with the right hand. In some cases, the violinist will pluck strings with the left hand. This is done to facilitate transitions from pizzicato to arco playing. It is also used in some virtuoso showpieces. Left hand pizzicato is usually done on open strings. Pizzicato is used on all of the violin family instruments; however, the systematic study of advanced pizzicato techniques is most developed in [jazz bass](/wiki/Jazz_bass "Jazz bass"), a style in which the instrument is almost exclusively plucked.

The right arm, hand, and bow and the bow speed are responsible for tone quality, [rhythm](/wiki/Rhythm "Rhythm"), [dynamics](/wiki/Dynamics_(music) "Dynamics (music)"), [articulation](/wiki/Articulation_(music) "Articulation (music)"), and most (but not all) changes in [timbre](/wiki/Timbre "Timbre"). The player draws the bow over the string, causing the string to vibrate and produce a sustained tone. The bow is a wooden stick with tensioned horsetail hair, which has been rosined with a bar of [rosin](/wiki/Rosin "Rosin"). The natural texture of the horsehair and the stickiness of the rosin help the bow to "grip" the string, and thus when the bow is drawn over the string, the bow causes the string to sound a pitch.

Bowing can be used to produce long sustained notes or melodies. With a [string section](/wiki/String_section "String section"), if the players in a section change their bows at different times, a note can seem to be endlessly sustainable. As well, the bow can be used to play short, crisp little notes, such as repeated notes, scales and arpeggios, which provide a propulsive rhythm in many styles of music.

#### Bowing techniques

The most essential part of bowing technique is the bow grip. It is usually with the thumb bent in the small area between the frog and the winding of the bow. The other fingers are spread somewhat evenly across the top part of the bow. The pinky finger is curled with the tip of the finger placed on the wood next to the screw. The violin produces louder notes with greater bow speed or more weight on the string. The two methods are not equivalent, because they produce different timbres; pressing down on the string tends to produce a harsher, more intense sound. One can also achieve a louder sound by placing the bow closer to the bridge.

The sounding point where the bow intersects the string also influences timbre (or "tone colour"). Playing close to the bridge (*sul ponticello*) gives a more intense sound than usual, emphasizing the higher harmonics; and playing with the bow over the end of the fingerboard (*sul tasto*) makes for a delicate, ethereal sound, emphasizing the [fundamental frequency](/wiki/Fundamental_frequency "Fundamental frequency"). [Shinichi Suzuki](/wiki/Shinichi_Suzuki_(violinist) "Shinichi Suzuki (violinist)") referred to the sounding point as the *[Kreisler](/wiki/Fritz_Kreisler "Fritz Kreisler") highway*; one may think of different sounding points as *lanes* in the highway.

Various methods of attack with the bow produce [different articulations](/wiki/Playing_the_violin#Bowing "Playing the violin"). There are many bowing techniques that allow for every range of playing style. Many teachers, players, and orchestras spend a lot of time developing techniques and creating a unified technique within the group. These techniques include legato-style bowing (a smooth, connected, sustained sound suitable for melodies), [collé](/wiki/Coll%C3%A9_(violin) "Collé (violin)"), and a variety of bowings which produce shorter notes, including ricochet, [sautillé](/wiki/Sautill%C3%A9_(bow_stroke) "Sautillé (bow stroke)"), [martelé](/wiki/Martel%C3%A9_(bowstroke) "Martelé (bowstroke)"), [spiccato](/wiki/Spiccato "Spiccato"), and staccato.

#### Pizzicato

A note marked *pizz.* (abbreviation for *[pizzicato](/wiki/Pizzicato "Pizzicato")*) in the written music is to be played by plucking the string with a finger of the right hand rather than by bowing. (The index finger is most commonly used here.) Sometimes in orchestra parts or virtuoso solo music where the bow hand is occupied (or for show-off effect), *left-hand pizzicato* will be indicated by a + (plus sign) below or above the note. In left-hand pizzicato, two fingers are put on the string; one (usually the index or middle finger) is put on the correct note, and the other (usually the ring finger or little finger) is put above the note. The higher finger then plucks the string while the lower one stays on, thus producing the correct pitch. By increasing the force of the pluck, one can increase the volume of the note that the string is producing. Pizzicato is used in orchestral works and in solo showpieces. In orchestral parts, violinists often have to make very quick shifts from arco to pizzicato, and vice versa.

#### Col legno

A marking of *[col legno](/wiki/Col_legno "Col legno")* ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") for "with the wood") in the written music calls for striking the string(s) with the stick of the bow, rather than by drawing the hair of the bow across the strings. This bowing technique is somewhat rarely used, and results in a muted percussive sound. The eerie quality of a violin section playing *col legno* is exploited in some symphonic pieces, notably the "Witches' Dance" of the last movement of [Berlioz's](/wiki/Hector_Berlioz "Hector Berlioz") [Symphonie Fantastique](/wiki/Symphonie_Fantastique "Symphonie Fantastique"). [Saint-Saëns](/wiki/Saint-Sa%C3%ABns "Saint-Saëns")'s symphonic poem [*Danse Macabre*](/wiki/Danse_macabre_(Saint-Sa%C3%ABns) "Danse macabre (Saint-Saëns)") includes the string section using the *col legno* technique to imitate the sound of dancing skeletons. "Mars" from Gustav Holst's "[The Planets](/wiki/The_Planets "The Planets")" uses *col legno* to play a repeated rhythm in **5**  
**4** time signature. [Benjamin Britten](/wiki/Benjamin_Britten "Benjamin Britten")'s *[The Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra](/wiki/The_Young_Person%27s_Guide_to_the_Orchestra "The Young Person's Guide to the Orchestra")* demands its use in the "[Percussion](/wiki/Percussion "Percussion")" Variation. [Dmitri Shostakovich](/wiki/Dmitri_Shostakovich "Dmitri Shostakovich") uses it in his Fourteenth Symphony in the movement 'At the Sante Jail'. Some violinists, however, object to this style of playing as it can damage the finish and impair the value of a fine bow, but most of such will compromise by using a cheap bow for at least the duration of the passage in question.

#### Detaché

A smooth and even stroke during which bow speed and weight are the same from beginning of the stroke to the end.[[38]](#cite_note-40)

#### Martelé

Literally *hammered*, a strongly accented effect produced by releasing each bowstroke forcefully and suddenly. Martelé can be played in any part of the bow. It is sometimes indicated in written music by an arrowhead.

#### Tremolo

[Tremolo](/wiki/Tremolo "Tremolo") is the very rapid repetition (typically of a single note, but occasionally of multiple notes), usually played at the tip of the bow. Tremolo is marked with three short, slanted lines across the stem of the note. Tremolo is often used as a [sound effect](/wiki/Sound_effect "Sound effect") in orchestral music, particularly in the [Romantic music era](/wiki/Romantic_music_era "Romantic music era") (1800–1910) and in opera music.

#### Mute or *sordino*

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Two_mutes.jpg/250px-Two_mutes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Two_mutes.jpg)

*Ad hoc* clothespin mute and a rubber practice mute

Attaching a small metal, rubber, leather, or wooden device called a *[mute](/wiki/Mute_(music) "Mute (music)")*, or *sordino*, to the bridge of the violin gives a softer, more mellow tone, with fewer audible [overtones](/wiki/Overtone "Overtone"); the sound of an entire orchestral string section playing with mutes has a hushed quality. The mute changes both the loudness and the [timbre](/wiki/Timbre "Timbre") ("tone colour") of a violin. The conventional Italian markings for mute usage are *con sord.*, or *con sordino*, meaning 'with mute'; and *senza sord.*, meaning 'without mute'; or *via sord.*, meaning 'mute off'.

Larger metal, rubber, or wooden mutes are widely available, known as *practice mutes* or *hotel mutes*. Such mutes are generally not used in performance, but are used to deaden the sound of the violin in practice areas such as hotel rooms. (For practicing purposes there is also the [mute violin](/wiki/Mute_violin "Mute violin"), a violin without a sound box.) Some composers have used practice mutes for special effect, for example, at the end of [Luciano Berio](/wiki/Luciano_Berio "Luciano Berio")'s *Sequenza VIII* for solo violin.

## Musical styles

Main article: [Violin in music](/wiki/Violin_in_music "Violin in music")

### Classical music

A [sonata](/wiki/Sonata "Sonata") for two violins by the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music") composer [Telemann](/wiki/Georg_Philipp_Telemann "Georg Philipp Telemann"). A relatively typical baroque violin composition, it would probably have been performed with less use of [vibrato](/wiki/Vibrato "Vibrato") originally.



The *[Meditation](/wiki/M%C3%A9ditation_(Tha%C3%AFs) "Méditation (Thaïs)")* from Massenet's opera *Thais*. The very [legato](/wiki/Legato "Legato") style of playing, with lavish use of [portamento](/wiki/Portamento "Portamento"), [rubato](/wiki/Rubato "Rubato") and [vibrato](/wiki/Vibrato "Vibrato") and the higher registers of the instrument is typical of violin playing in the late [Romantic](/wiki/Romantic_music "Romantic music") period.

Since the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque_music "Baroque music") era, the violin has been one of the most important of all instruments in [classical music](/wiki/European_classical_music "European classical music"), for several reasons. The tone of the violin stands out above other instruments, making it appropriate for playing a melody line. In the hands of a good player, the violin is extremely agile, and can execute rapid and difficult sequences of notes.

Violins make up a large part of an [orchestra](/wiki/Orchestra "Orchestra"), and are usually divided into two sections, known as the first and second violins. Composers often assign the melody to the first violins, typically a more difficult part using higher positions. In contrast, second violins play harmony, accompaniment patterns or the melody an octave lower than the first violins. A [string quartet](/wiki/String_quartet "String quartet") similarly has parts for first and second violins, as well as a [viola](/wiki/Viola "Viola") part, and a bass instrument, such as the [cello](/wiki/Cello "Cello") or, rarely, the [double bass](/wiki/Double_bass "Double bass").

### Jazz

The earliest references to [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") [performance using the violin](/wiki/Jazz_violin "Jazz violin") as a solo instrument are documented during the first decades of the 20th century. [Joe Venuti](/wiki/Joe_Venuti "Joe Venuti"), one of the first jazz violinists, is known for his work with guitarist [Eddie Lang](/wiki/Eddie_Lang "Eddie Lang") during the 1920s. Since that time there have been many [improvising violinists](/wiki/List_of_jazz_violinists "List of jazz violinists") including [Stéphane Grappelli](/wiki/St%C3%A9phane_Grappelli "Stéphane Grappelli"), [Stuff Smith](/wiki/Stuff_Smith "Stuff Smith"), [Eddie South](/wiki/Eddie_South "Eddie South"), [Regina Carter](/wiki/Regina_Carter "Regina Carter"), [Johnny Frigo](/wiki/Johnny_Frigo "Johnny Frigo"), [John Blake](/wiki/John_Blake,_Jr. "John Blake, Jr."), [Adam Taubitz](/wiki/Adam_Taubitz "Adam Taubitz"), [Leroy Jenkins](/wiki/Leroy_Jenkins_(jazz_musician) "Leroy Jenkins (jazz musician)"), and [Jean-Luc Ponty](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Ponty "Jean-Luc Ponty"). While not primarily jazz violinists, [Darol Anger](/wiki/Darol_Anger "Darol Anger") and [Mark O'Connor](/wiki/Mark_O%27Connor "Mark O'Connor") have spent significant parts of their careers playing jazz.
The Swiss-Cuban violinist [Yilian Cañizares](/wiki/Yilian_Ca%C3%B1izares "Yilian Cañizares") mixes jazz with Cuban music.[[39]](#cite_note-NZZ-41)

Violins also appear in ensembles supplying orchestral backgrounds to many jazz recordings.

### Indian classical music

The Indian violin, while essentially the same instrument as that used in Western music, is different in some senses.[[40]](#cite_note-FirstPost-42) The instrument is tuned so that the IV and III strings (G and D on a western-tuned violin) and the II and I (A and E) strings are sa–pa (do–sol) pairs and sound the same but are offset by an octave, resembling common scordatura or fiddle cross-tunings such as G3–D4–G4–D5 or A3–E4–A4–E5. The tonic sa (do) is not fixed, but variably tuned to accommodate the vocalist or lead player. The way the musician holds the instrument varies from Western to Indian music. In Indian music the musician sits on the floor cross-legged with the right foot out in front of them. The scroll of the instrument rests on the foot. This position is essential to playing well due to the nature of Indian music. The hand can move all over the fingerboard and there is no set position for the left hand, so it is important for the violin to be in a steady, unmoving position.

### Popular music

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Lindsey_Stirling.jpg/250px-Lindsey_Stirling.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lindsey_Stirling.jpg)

[Lindsey Stirling](/wiki/Lindsey_Stirling "Lindsey Stirling") performing at [TEDx](/wiki/TEDx "TEDx") Berkeley, 2012

Up through at least the 1970s, most types of popular music used bowed [string sections](/wiki/String_section "String section"). They were extensively used in popular music throughout the 1920s and early 1930s. With the rise of [swing music](/wiki/Swing_music "Swing music"), however, from 1935 to 1945, the string sound was often used to add to the fullness of [big band](/wiki/Big_band "Big band") music. Following the [swing era](/wiki/Swing_era "Swing era"), from the late 1940s to the mid-1950s, strings began to be revived in [traditional pop](/wiki/Traditional_pop "Traditional pop") music. This trend accelerated in the late 1960s, with a significant revival of the use of strings, especially in [soul music](/wiki/Soul_music "Soul music"). Popular [Motown](/wiki/Motown "Motown") recordings of the late 1960s and 1970s relied heavily on strings as part of their trademark texture.

With the rise of [electronically created music](/wiki/Electronic_music "Electronic music") in the 1980s, violins declined in use, as synthesized string sounds played by a keyboardist with a [synthesizer](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer") took their place. However, while the violin has had very little usage in mainstream [rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music"), it has some history in [progressive rock](/wiki/Progressive_rock "Progressive rock") (e.g., [Electric Light Orchestra](/wiki/Electric_Light_Orchestra "Electric Light Orchestra"), [King Crimson](/wiki/King_Crimson "King Crimson"), [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas_(band) "Kansas (band)"), [Gentle Giant](/wiki/Gentle_Giant "Gentle Giant")). The instrument has a stronger place in modern [jazz fusion](/wiki/Jazz_fusion "Jazz fusion") bands, notably [The Corrs](/wiki/The_Corrs "The Corrs").

The popularity of [crossover music](/wiki/Crossover_music "Crossover music") beginning in the last years of the 20th century has brought the violin back into the popular music arena, with both electric and acoustic violins being used by popular bands. [Dave Matthews Band](/wiki/Dave_Matthews_Band "Dave Matthews Band") features violinist [Boyd Tinsley](/wiki/Boyd_Tinsley "Boyd Tinsley"). [The Flock](/wiki/The_Flock_(band) "The Flock (band)") featured violinist [Jerry Goodman](/wiki/Jerry_Goodman "Jerry Goodman") who later joined the [jazz-rock fusion](/wiki/Jazz-rock_fusion "Jazz-rock fusion") band, [The Mahavishnu Orchestra](/wiki/The_Mahavishnu_Orchestra "The Mahavishnu Orchestra"). [James](/wiki/James_(band) "James (band)")' [Saul Davies](/wiki/Saul_Davies "Saul Davies"), who is also a [guitarist](/wiki/Guitar "Guitar"), was enlisted by the band as a violinist. [Vanessa-Mae](/wiki/Vanessa-Mae "Vanessa-Mae") is known for fusing classical and popular genres, which she calls "violin techno-acoustic fusion", with her success seeing her named the wealthiest entertainer under 30 in the UK in 2006.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

The [folk metal](/wiki/Folk_metal "Folk metal") band [Ithilien](/wiki/Ithilien_(band) "Ithilien (band)") use violin extensively along their discography.[[42]](#cite_note-44) [Progressive metal](/wiki/Progressive_metal "Progressive metal") band [Ne Obliviscaris](/wiki/Ne_Obliviscaris_(band) "Ne Obliviscaris (band)") feature a violin player, Tim Charles, in their line-up.[[43]](#cite_note-45)

Independent artists, such as [Owen Pallett](/wiki/Owen_Pallett "Owen Pallett"), [The Shondes](/wiki/The_Shondes "The Shondes"), and [Andrew Bird](/wiki/Andrew_Bird "Andrew Bird"), have also spurred increased interest in the instrument.[[44]](#cite_note-ChicagoMag-46) [Indie](/wiki/Independent_music "Independent music") bands have often embraced new and unusual arrangements, allowing them more freedom to feature the violin than many mainstream musical artists. [Lindsey Stirling](/wiki/Lindsey_Stirling "Lindsey Stirling") plays the violin in conjunction with electronic/dubstep/trance rifts and beats.[[45]](#cite_note-hercampus-47)

[Eric Stanley](/wiki/Eric_Stanley_(violinist) "Eric Stanley (violinist)") improvises on the violin with [hip hop music](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music")/pop/classical elements and instrumental beats.[[46]](#cite_note-vh1-48)[[47]](#cite_note-49) The successful [indie rock](/wiki/Indie_rock "Indie rock") and [baroque pop](/wiki/Baroque_pop "Baroque pop") band [Arcade Fire](/wiki/Arcade_Fire "Arcade Fire") use violins extensively in their arrangements.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

### Folk music and fiddling

Main article: [Fiddle](/wiki/Fiddle "Fiddle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Anders_Zorn_-_Hins_Anders_%281904%29.jpg/250px-Anders_Zorn_-_Hins_Anders_%281904%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Anders_Zorn_-_Hins_Anders_(1904).jpg)

The [fiddler](/wiki/Fiddler "Fiddler") Hins Anders Ersson painted by [Anders Zorn](/wiki/Anders_Zorn "Anders Zorn"), 1904

Like many other instruments used in [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music"), the violin descends from remote ancestors that were used for [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"). Following a stage of intensive development in the late [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"), largely in [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), the violin had improved (in volume, tone, and agility), to the point that it not only became a very important instrument in art music, but proved highly appealing to folk musicians as well, ultimately spreading very widely, sometimes displacing earlier bowed instruments. [Ethnomusicologists](/wiki/Ethnomusicology "Ethnomusicology") have observed its widespread use in Europe, Asia, and the Americas.

When played as a folk instrument, the violin is usually referred to in English as a *fiddle* (although the term *fiddle* can be used informally no matter what the genre of music). Worldwide, there are various stringed instruments such as the [wheel fiddle](/wiki/Hurdy-gurdy "Hurdy-gurdy") and [Apache fiddle](/wiki/Apache_fiddle "Apache fiddle") that are also called "fiddles". Fiddle music differs from classical in that the tunes are generally considered dance music,[[49]](#cite_note-OKHistory-51) and various techniques, such as droning, shuffling, and ornamentation specific to particular styles are used. In many traditions of folk music, the tunes are not written but are memorized by successive generations of musicians and passed on[[49]](#cite_note-OKHistory-51) in what is known as the [oral tradition](/wiki/Oral_tradition "Oral tradition"). Many [old-time](/wiki/Old-time_music "Old-time music") pieces call for [cross-tuning](/wiki/Cross_tuning "Cross tuning"), or using tunings other than standard GDAE. Some players of American styles of folk fiddling (such as [bluegrass](/wiki/Bluegrass_music "Bluegrass music") or old-time) have their bridge's top edge cut to a slightly flatter curve, making techniques such as a "double shuffle" less taxing on the bow arm, as it reduces the range of motion needed for alternating between [double stops](/wiki/Double_stop "Double stop") on different string pairs. Fiddlers who use solid steel core strings may prefer to use a [tailpiece](/wiki/Tailpiece "Tailpiece") with fine tuners on all four strings, instead of the single fine tuner on the E string used by many classical players.

### Arabic music

As well as the Arabic [rababah](/wiki/Rebab "Rebab"), the violin has been used in Arabic music.

## Electric violins

Main article: [Electric violin](/wiki/Electric_violin "Electric violin")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Transformation.JPG/330px-Transformation.JPG)](/wiki/File:Transformation.JPG)

Acoustic and electric violins

**Electric violins** have a [magnetic](/wiki/Magnetism "Magnetism") or [piezoelectric](/wiki/Piezoelectricity "Piezoelectricity") [pickup](/wiki/Pickup_(music_technology) "Pickup (music technology)") that converts string vibration to an electric signal. A [patch cable](/wiki/Patch_cable "Patch cable") or wireless transmitter sends the signal to an amplifier of a [PA system](/wiki/PA_system "PA system"). Electric violins are usually constructed as such, but a pickup can be added to a conventional acoustic violin. An electric violin with a resonating body that produces listening-level sound independently of the electric elements can be called an *electro-acoustic violin*. To be effective as an acoustic violin, electro-acoustic violins retain much of the resonating body of the violin, and often resemble an acoustic violin or fiddle. The body may be finished in bright colors and made from alternative materials to wood. These violins may need to be hooked up to an [instrument amplifier](/wiki/Instrument_amplifier "Instrument amplifier") or [PA system](/wiki/PA_system "PA system"). Some types come with a silent option that allows the player to use headphones that are hooked up to the violin. The first specially built [electric violins](/wiki/Electric_violin "Electric violin") date back to 1928 and were made by Victor Pfeil, Oskar Vierling, George Eisenberg, [Benjamin Miessner](/wiki/Benjamin_Miessner "Benjamin Miessner"), [George Beauchamp](/wiki/George_Beauchamp "George Beauchamp"), [Hugo Benioff](/wiki/Hugo_Benioff "Hugo Benioff") and Fredray Kislingbury. These violins can be plugged into [effect units](/wiki/Effect_unit "Effect unit"), just like an [electric guitar](/wiki/Electric_guitar "Electric guitar"), including [distortion](/wiki/Distortion_(music) "Distortion (music)"), [wah-wah pedal](/wiki/Wah-wah_pedal "Wah-wah pedal") and [reverb](/wiki/Reverb "Reverb"). Since electric violins do not rely on string tension and resonance to amplify their sound, they can have more strings. For example, five-stringed electric violins are available from several manufacturers, and a seven-string electric violin (with three lower strings encompassing the [cello](/wiki/Cello "Cello")'s range) is also available.[[50]](#cite_note-52) The majority of the first [electric violinists](/wiki/Electric_violinist "Electric violinist") were musicians playing [jazz fusion](/wiki/Jazz_fusion "Jazz fusion") (e.g., [Jean-Luc Ponty](/wiki/Jean-Luc_Ponty "Jean-Luc Ponty")) and popular music.

## Violin authentication

Main article: [Violin authentication](/wiki/Violin_authentication "Violin authentication")

Violin authentication is the process of determining the maker and manufacture date of a violin. This can be an important process as significant value may be attached to violins made either by specific makers or at specific times and locations. Analysis on design, model, wood characteristics, and varnish texture can be used to authenticate a violin.[[51]](#cite_note-53) [Forgery](/wiki/Art_forgery "Art forgery") and other methods of [fraudulent](/wiki/Fraud "Fraud") misrepresentation can be used to inflate the value of an instrument.

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-1)** In short, the difference between a fiddle and a violin is simply in the style of music they are used to play. Violin players typically perform [classical music](/wiki/Classical_music "Classical music"), such as in [orchestras](/wiki/Orchestras "Orchestras") and [string quartets](/wiki/String_quartets "String quartets"). Fiddle players, on the other hand, typically perform [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), and other types of traditional music. Violinists hold the bow at its extreme end, so as to use its full length, whereas some fiddlers hold it higher up, to facilitate quick movements and "jumps".
2. **[^](#cite_ref-2)** Violins with six, seven, or more strings exist, but are rare, and must be amplified electronically.
